sentences.text: Yes.
The reason I'm bringing him in is because he has played a part, a very important part, in my life.
[CLEARS THROAT]
So when he was 18 years old and I was-- 18 and I was 15, he, he brought me down one day said, can I go with you down the hill?
I said, it isn't winter.
It isn't slippery.
[CLEARS THROAT] And I said, I, I-- you know I'm going to get in trouble with my father.
He doesn't allow that.
And-- [CLEARS THROAT]
When I came-- when I came down, we came by a, a-- those old fashioned water things.
What do you call them?
That you pour out the water.
He is-- I said, I have to say goodbye to you, because that's too close to home.
It's already my house.
It's the next one after this one, and I don't want my father and mother to see and be griefed about it, I said.
Because this was actually the worst thing about it.
They were griefed about it.
And so he placed a kiss on my lips very quickly, and he ran away.
And then he told me, before the kiss, he told me, I, I signed up for the military.
I'm going into Krakow.
I'm going to be in Krakow.
Can I write you?
Can I write to you?
I said not to my house, you can't.
I said, no way.
My father sees correspondence with you, oh, he would just-- they would die and would kill me, I said.
And he said, suppose I write it to my mother and my two sisters.
He didn't have a father.
Father died in World War I.
[CLEARS THROAT] And he said, I write it to my sisters, and my mother and my sisters love you, and they're going to bring you the letters on the marketplace anyway.
And I said, OK.
If you think this way.
But I don't know even if even I can answer you.
Oh, yes you can.
You don't have to-- you give mom the letters and she's going to-- my mom, he says-- and she's going to mail them for me.
And that's what we did after this.
He made-- But anyway, this is the boy-- [CLEARS THROAT] --that went away and then send me-- send me money to come to Krakow when he had already the stripes and he was an officer.
This is years later.
I was, at that time, 18, and he was-- that's the year before the war-- and he was 21.
And he said he would want me to come then and marry me.
We did correspond.
It wasn't so much about love talking.
How are you doing?
How are your parents?
And things like this, and love you at the end, and I wrote down love you too, but I didn't know it was that serious.
And I said-- once he asked me before, I said, not in a million years.
This would kill my parents, I said.
I couldn't do that.
And then he asked me again.
I want you to know, he said-- in the whole letter he said that you can-- I'll buy you a silver candelabra.
And you're going to light the candles just like your mama does, he says.
And you can bring up the children in the Jewish faith.
And if this doesn't work, he said, I'm sending you the money to come to Krakow, because I'm entitled to an apartment and we could get married now.
And, and the children, you can bring up, he said, and you can do everything as you did at home.
And if worse comes to worse, [INAUDIBLE] I'll have myself circumcised and accept the Jewish religion.
So I received the letters, previous ones from the mother or the sisters, and this one, too.
I didn't even-- didn't even tell a word about it to my parents.
Just telling him about it, about the correspondence, would already hurt them terribly.
I didn't do anything.
So I just sent the money back.
And I said, I'm very sorry if I led you on or something, and I can't marry you never.
I can't.
I can't do that to my family.
Not in a million years.
So this was the end of it.
And then comes the war.
He was a pilot, and he was fighting.
He was-- he was escaped to Russia, escape from Russia back home.
When he came home, he was doing underground work bringing officers.

Considering the borders are closed, this was the best connection between Czechoslovakia from Poland, for Polish officers to escape, from Poland to Czechoslovakia, and they had another organization, the same there.
And then to Hungary, from Hungary, I think, to Italy, and then to England, and formed an army in, in, in England.
And so he was bringing over the officers because he knew the borders so well just from before, he used to ski, and so he knew the whole territory well.
And everybody in town that wasn't German or SS knew that he was doing that work, or many people did.
So my father knew too.
And when that officer came that night, the next morning, my father said, you're gonna go to Czechoslovakia.
You have to go.
You have to escape.
Because he can come back tomorrow again and, and I don't want to live, Mom says, to watch something like this.
He was drunk, and he can do just about anything.
He'll shoot us all, too.
I said, well, there is no choice.
And then my father said, and-- I'm going to-- no.
He didn't say anything.
He just asked one of the neighbor boys, a Jewish boy, send over to-- he was home in civilian clothes.
The military, the generals, Andrej was home.
They didn't know anything about him doing that work, naturally.
And send over a little boy.
He said, would you go over and ask and Andrej Garbera to come to the Kornreich's home.
And the same-- the same morning he came to the Kornreich's home, Andrej walked in, didn't say anything to me.
Father shook his hand, first time in his life.
And my father had-- must have loved me a lot to, after not talking to a gentile boy, to ask him, would you please bring my daughter.
I know you're doing that kind of work, and I know she'll be safe with you.
Would you bring her to Czechoslovakia tonight?
And he said, sure I will.
I haven't got no money to pay you, he said.
Because sometimes he got paid if they had money.
If they didn't, he brought them without money.
He, said Mr.
Kornreich, we are friends since childhood, since we were little children.
We played when we were little children.
Then teenagers, we didn't, and then now as the time he's a grown up.
And he said, no.
If we were childhood friends, and I wouldn't take it if you had the money, Mr.
Kornreich.
And they-- father offered him a cigarette for the very few that he still had.
They sat down had a cigarette.
I wasn't consulted, wasn't told anything about it.
Nothing.
This is the Hasidic family style.
They make arrangements.
And but father knew that I wouldn't object, because it was my life saving, and it's definitely with him, I wouldn't object.
And he said, Mr.
Kornreich-- he shook his hand when, when he left-- I give you my word of honor as an officer that, as far as I can help it, not a hair is going to fall off her head, he said.
And I'm not going to touch her more than holding her hand, because the territory is rough.
Am I allowed to do that?
Yes, father said.
And this was-- this was the last time that we-- that I talked to him.
The last time I talked to him when he brought us to Czechoslovakia.
Not the last time.
He came another couple of times afterwards.
He came one time with a package.
I-- he brought me to a farmer that was working for underground, and the farmer was friendly with German officers and played cards with them at night to cover it up for a cover up.
Just pretend.
So the officers had no idea that he was working for the underground.

In the morning, the farmer-- I got dressed in farmer's clothes and brought to my uncle, and the next day, Andrej came to bring me clothes from my mother, because I was only in the jacket I had and, and a pair of boots I was wearing for the muddy road, and nothing else with me.
So he brought some clothes and, I think, a big package, brought it.
He risked his life again to bring it all the way to Czechoslovakia, to the city, and then he came one more time to tell me--
[SNEEZE]
--that he-- one more time, he came to tell me, can I talk to you, he said.
He came to the door with something from home.
Can I talk to you?
I said, I don't want my uncle and my aunt to see we are talking.
I said, this is embarrassing.
You know how they are, the old Hasidic Jews and, and they have objection to it.
I'm sorry, I said, but I can't.
He said, I've got something very important to tell you.
I said, OK.
Let's go here, a little bit to the side where my aunt can't see it.
And he says, my brother lives in Czechoslovakia.
I knew his brother.
He's married to this Slovakian girl.
He lives only 15 kilometers from there, which is 15 kilometers, like, eight, 10, 10 miles, something.
And they have a nice, big house and they have dedicated a big room, a separate room.
I heard rumors they're going to take Jews to, to, to camps, to work camps.
And but if-- [CLEARS THROAT]
--if they're mixed marriages, they're not going to take.
My brother left, gave me that room, and gave me the opportunity to have a little stove there and everything.
Can I marry you?
If I marry you, I can save your life, he said.
I said, I still can't do it.
But your parents-- you don't even know if your parents are going to be tomorrow alive.
They are there in Poland, and you know what they're doing there.
Don't you want to save your life?
I said, not at a price of, of what they're going to pay for it.
I said, I'm sorry.
I can't.
So he left, and about one time-- one other time I saw him in Czechoslovakia, and I didn't talk to him because I had a Jewish boy that I met and was going with the Jewish boy.
My uncle arranged it and my father and mother were happy about it, so I did it.
But I told that boy that I had somebody that I really liked a lot, or loved.
By that time, I was grown up enough to tell him, yes, I did love him too.
And-- [CLEARS THROAT]
I didn't acknowledge, even, his presence there when he passed by in the street, which I'm sorry to this day that I didn't, because he saved my life and I didn't even say that.
I didn't know what the Jewish boy was going to feel like.
And so he said-- but my face was red, so he said, who is that boy.
Is that the one?
I told him I can't marry you, the Jewish boy, because I love somebody else.
And he said, was that the boy?
I say, yes.
Why didn't you go over to him and say thank you or say hello to him?
I said, well, I didn't know how you were going to take it.
And he said, I-- like a normal human being, he said.
Like somebody grateful that he saved your life.
I said-- so I looked around, and the street was empty.
And about a couple of months later, he was dead on one of those trips.
He, he, he was hiding on a tree, and he froze and had pneumonia, and he died.
So this is the last time, on the street, that I saw him, is Andrej.
And then, then I came home after this.
A couple of months later, I came home because I heard rumors they're going to displace all the Jews away from Tylicz, because Tylicz and [?
Welshinka ?] are too close to the-- to the Slovakian border.
And apparently, some Jews escaped or something, and they probably noticed me, too, disappearing, there in Russia.
And they don't trust the Jews anymore.
And you can go anywhere you want to-- [COUGH] Excuse me.
[COUGHING] [DRINKS] 25 kilometers away from the border.
You can take with you-- we-- remember, we dug out potatoes and there was a full cellar of potatoes still in the winter.
It's in the fall the next year, but there were still some potatoes there.
[CLEARS THROAT] And, and you can-- when I came home.
I had a guide, another guide hired, my uncle hired, and then I came home, because their parents were elderly.
And I said, I can do a lot more for them.
And I can sew to earn some money too, for, for a living.
[CLEARS THROAT] And then you can take one loaf of bread and you can take one change of clothes, you can take 500 zlotys.
But the zlotys wasn't a problem because we didn't have 500 zlotys.
We had a little bit less than that.
But a change of clothes and things like this.
So I sneaked in the sewing machine under the clothes and under the storage we take with the horse and buggy.

The cows, everything else, the potatoes in the cellar, everything that we possessed, all the clothes in the wardrobe, everything, jewelry or whatever we had, nothing we could take with us, and to leave the key in the door.
So we left.
I went back one day.
One day, I went back on foot.
This was like 20, 30 kilometers.

I went back by foot to ask the sheriff from the town, to risk my life because the Germans were there-- I wasn't supposed to be there-- to ask the sheriff to-- I wore a babushka with the long braids and the clothes like they wear more, not what I was wearing, and ask him if he let me take one cow, because the farm we were living in, we gave him all the money we had.
[CLEARS THROAT] And then we had nothing else to buy food or anything.
So at least we have some milk, that I can have a cow.
He gave me permission, and he gave me a signed paper that I'm allowed to take the cow, because there were Germans going on trucks and back and forth.
One of them passing me by said-- they did see.
I had-- I was wearing the band, then, with the Star of David.
And they said, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
So meaning, we're going to conquer the whole world, and Jerusalem too.
My heart was going like this.
To-- we'll see you in Jerusalem, he said.
So it means they're going to conquer the world.
So I just drudged on farther, stopped in one Jewish home on the-- in the halfway, on the way home with this one after this.
And they let me keep the cow there overnight, and they gave me some food, and I could sleep there.
Then I brought the cow to that farmer, and-- which one would you like?
To finish this story?
Yeah?
With the farmer there?
And I started sewing on that sewing machine that I took with me, and there was some guides that brought over-- [?
Tolek ?] is his name.
A gentile guide from [INAUDIBLE].
And [?
Tolek ?] brought Danka over to Czechoslovakia.
And with, with Esther, the little girl from my sister.
Sister Zosia, she had two, two children, the little boy that needed surgery, and Esther was six years old, she was still-- or seven by that time.
She was still at home with my grandparents and with, with us.
And then Danka-- when Danka brought her over-- brought her over-- after me, Danka brought her over, and she stayed awhile in Czechoslovakia.
When both of us came back.
The little girl stayed with her mother.
We sent her to Bratislava.
She stayed with Zosia.
Zosia had two, two children with her, and she was working for a Jewish family, a, a, a widower and a son, as a housekeeper.
And she could keep the children in there.
And, and we stayed with the parents, and we moved to that town.
And I started sewing and earning some money.
But the guys who brought her over, none of them charged a penny.
So they didn't know us at all.
So you see?
We were living with the gentiles, something that I want to emphasize because they deserve it.
You have to acknowledge it.
At least I think so.
My conscience tells me so.
And two different ones, or three different guys between Danka and the little girl [INAUDIBLE] over.
But then bringing her go back and bringing me back, there were-- there were three different boys.
And they didn't take any money for it.
In fact, one of them, [?
Tolek, ?] came over some day, and he asked what we're living from.
And I said, well, I earned some money.
I try to earn some money here with sewing, but, but it's very little.
So one day, he came over with a box full of almonds.
He said, I got these from Czechoslovakia, but they were very cheap.
I could get a lot of them-- that's what he was telling me to make me feel good-- and you go to [INAUDIBLE] to the Jewish families so you can sell them.
So several times, he brought over almonds.
I have two quick questions.
Can you give me a time frame?
When is all this happening, going back and forth from Czechoslovakia?
OK.
We were-- I was the first time in Czechoslovakia-- let's see, we moved in September, October, November, December.
December I went to Czechoslovakia.
In the spring, I came back because of the rumors, OK?
In the spring of 1940.
In the fall of 1940, we are sent away.
We are displaced from our home.
We're sent away.
And we are in the fall of 1940, I am sewing, to the part that I just came and trying to earn-- and he brought the almonds, and I sell some almonds were there.

And suddenly, there were rumors that in the fall of 1940, rumors that-- [CLEARS THROAT] They're going to-- they took some Jewish girls somewhere in another town to the military compound and used them as, as prostitutes there.
Well, mom got up one morning.
Mom heard it, or there was some [INAUDIBLE] Jewish newspaper or something that she read it somehow.
She said, listen.
This is what's going on, and you two are going to Czechoslovakia again.
I hired Tolek.
I said, mom, I'm not leaving.
I'm sewing, and I sell almonds or something.
And we can live now, and I can work for it.
And I can milk the cow in the morning.
Because sometimes the farmer milk the cow before I came down to milk it.
So I got up at 3:00 in the morning to try to milk the cow so we had some milk too.
The farmer was really mean.
That was in another town I told you, 28 kilometers, really mean.
And took all the money from us, and then he wanted the milk from the cow too.
But anyway, this one hated the Jews, [INAUDIBLE] anti-Semitic, but hated the Jews.
So I said, mom, you can't-- you're two elderly people.
Let's be honest, Mama.
I, I heard them whisper the night after this when I told her.
I heard them whisper.
The whole night, they were going back and forth.
And mom said, no.
Dad said, but what are we going to do?
I don't care.
I don't care.
So we're getting up in the morning.
I head the whole night they were talking about it.
I got up in the morning, and mom said, make yourself ready.
Tolek ?] is coming.
You're going to Czechoslovakia, both of you.
I said, mom, I can't do that.
And she said, OK.
Would you rather have to die, go away, you never see me again?
And I just die somewhere.
I don't care if it's in the street or in the woods or whatever.
I don't want to live to see my, my daughters raped, she said.
Well, I had no choice.
The sled came in about a half an hour later, and we packed up a little bundle with a few clothes, and we-- that's the last time we saw our parents.
[INAUDIBLE] riding away with the sled-- [CRYING] [DRINKS] Sorry.
They were running after the sled until they were specks of them just visit.
We were on the knees on the seat, Danka and I.
We were waving to them, and they were waving to us.
[CRYING] And that was the last time I saw them.
This is-- this is now winter 1940, and it's December, the end of December.
Excuse me.
And, and [?
Tolek ?] came, brought us over to some-- four or five more Jewish people from the town, from Tylicz, that were living in Florynka.
The place is called Florynka.
You need spelling of it?
Please.
Yeah?
F-L-O-R-Y-K-A, Florynka.
And-- so we leaving-- we left Florynka.
And then, OK.
[CLEARS THROAT] The-- after we came over the border, soon we-- my sister made arrangements with Jewish families to-- a well-to-do Jewish families in Bratislava.
That's where she was.
So we didn't go to the uncle anymore.
But we went to Bratislava where the sister was because we wanted to be together.
It was always a very closed knit family with lovely children.
We had wonderful parents.
I don't know how many times I mentioned it.
But you know by the father just letting me go to Czechoslovakia like this, must have loved us a lot.
And they really did.
And so we went to Bratislava.
I was staying with one family, Danka with another family that had a little boy.
So she was more nanny to the little boy.
And with this family, there was-- well-to-do family, they had a housekeeper, and they had a cook, and a cleaning woman.
I was the spice assistant.
I had the keys and always went with the woman to do shopping, the shopping.
And I watched the things that she was taking out there for the cooking and helping a little bit with the cooking there in Czechoslovakia.
And then there were rumors again they're going to take Jewish people, and especially young girls they're going to start taking.
And the family-- Stahler was their name, from the family that I was, with the Jewish family-- and said would be better-- [CLEARS THROAT] --that you be in small place.
And we got contacted some family.
I forgot to tell you, this spring-- that spring, [INAUDIBLE]-- this is already almost six months later, what I'm telling you now.

But before that, it was Passover, and I, I tried to call-- the first Passover when I was at my uncles, I sent over raisins that father could make the wine at home for Passover, and I send over matzos in a package.
And they received it.
OK.
But this is the second Passover I'm there, and I knew a Jewish tailor that was a sewing, forcefully sewing, for the-- by force-- sewing for the officers, leather coats.
And I knew him, and I said, I would like to call.
How do you go about it calling my parents and seeing if they're still there so I can send a package?
He said, well, I'm going to bribe one of the officers and tell him I give him two coats so that the other officers don't know about it-- or a coat for his wife or something.
[CLEARS THROAT] Excuse me.
And I make sure that he brings you telephone booths.
He brought me the telephone booths, he called Florynka, got the post office-- because in all those villages, there was a telephone in the post office.
And that was it, nobody had telephones.
Called Florynka, the post office.
When he got connected, he said, this is a German officer.
I like to speak to the chief of the-- the head of the post office.
They had [INAUDIBLE].
This is that.
And then he said, here.
You talking to her.
I'm going to give you a person.
Now, I want you to give her an honest answer, he said to him.
And I said, I'm Rena Kornreich and I-- in Florynka, this and this house-- I remembered the name of the farmer-- are living my parents, and I would like to speak to one of them.
Could you send somebody?
I can wait on the telephone.
Even though it's expensive.
He said you can wait.
And send a messenger to get him.

We waited about five minutes or 10 minutes, and the, the man on the other side of the telephone in Poland says, the messenger came back and said, in this house is only such and such family-- the owners of it-- and there is no Jewish family there.
And they don't know anything about it.
So this was the last time.
So I didn't-- this time I didn't get contact with them.

And I found out later after the war from Alex-- because all the people that left Tylicz were sort of in the same towns or near it-- that were taken all-- probably before, before Passover-- taken to a ghetto in Nowy Sacz somewhere, or [INAUDIBLE].
He doesn't know for sure either because he escaped to Czechoslovakia too.
But you were never in a ghetto?
Pardon me?
No.
I was not.
He was with his parents.
He escaped first to Czechoslovakia.
Then he came back.
And when he came back with his parents, then he escaped from the ghetto, Alex escaped.
The same Alex that, that came as a messenger.
Excuse me.
Is that allowed?
That came to tell-- to tell me that SS officer is going to come.
And he told me that they were all in a ghetto.
And either they-- assuming only things, rumors again-- they died.
They had the gassing vents.
And they took the Jews from the ghettos, if they wanted to get rid of them quickly, and they put them in vents and gassed them in the vents.
And they had another group digging the-- digging a hole.
So they threw them in, [INAUDIBLE] the hole.
Or they took them to Auschwitz, and they died there in the gas chambers.
I will never know.
But the last time they saw me was on that sled and waving to them.
And I can still see the picture, even the tiny little speck that we still looked at.
And I can't tell it without crying.
I'm sorry.
OK.
I'm now at the family in Bratislava.
And you-- you want me to spell Bratislava?
No?
In Bratislava, and they heard rumors that they going to take the young girls, that it will be better-- they contacted somebody, some Jewish committee probably, in a little town, Humenne.
Humenne.
I think I can.
Yeah.
H-U-M-E-NN-E.
Humenne.
As far as I remember.
I've seen it spelled with one N one time in that book that I-- but I think this is the correct, the correct name.
It's very close anyway.
If you say Humenne, people know where it was.
So they contacted their people.
And I didn't know in this same town were two girls-- three girls from my home town.
Erna Fela, that I met later in Auschwitz, and Dina.
Erna Fela [INAUDIBLE] and Dina Goldberger were in that same town.
So I had some friends there [? when ?] I came.
And I was assigned already from, from Bratislava to go to a Jewish family, and their name was Silber.
Silber I think is spelled like the German, S-I-B-E-R I think.
Silber.
S-I-L?
S-I-L-B-E-R, Silber.
Yeah.
And they had a five-year-old little girl, Gretchen.
And I stayed there and I helped a little bit in the house.
It was a young couple with a five-year-old little girl.
And I stayed there with them for-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is in the fall of '41 until the spring of '42.
Almost a year, but not a year.
And then big, big proclamations on every building and every office building, everything, from the Germans again.
Any foreigner that is here in town, the people who have foreigners, it's martial law from this moment on that you read that sign.
And the whole family, whoever is hiding the foreigners, is going to be shot to death and including the person that's hiding.
So you have a chance to give yourself up.
We're going to be at such and such at the military compound and such and such are the hours to come and report to us.
And you can then going to go to military camp and work for a while, and then you can go back.
That's what the sign said.
So I had the choice at that time to be shot to death myself and the young couple and the little girl.
Now, they were mad at me-- that they-- when I-- I tell you later when they were mad at me.
But anyway, so I gave myself up and went to the military compound.
I wrote a letter to my sister and wrote a letter to [?
Cheine ?] [?
Gottlob. ?] That was my boyfriend that I was supposed to marry in two weeks.
The wedding was scheduled, and the people that I lived with gave me the money to buy the material.
And a tailor made a robe for me and made some nightgowns.
Everything was preparations to have it before the holidays?
When-- yes.
I think before the holidays to get married.
And Danka said-- Danka was going to come.
And Zosia couldn't with the children.
But Danka was going to come to the wedding.
And I wrote a letter to him and wrote a letter to Danka and went to the military compound.
And I went in the afternoon dressed, prepared to it, without a coat.
Just the suit I was wearing and prepared to come back after I-- but you stay right here, he said.
And you stay right here was until the next morning outside with many other people there.
I didn't have any conversations with them because I was freezing.
I was holding myself, and I went on my knees, put down.
And I pulled my pleated skirt.
I had a pleated skirt and a checkered blazer.
And kept it over my legs.
I was cold, and I was falling off-- dozing off a little bit and that.
And I was thinking already, what did I do?
What did I do that I came here?
Was it the right thing to do?
And then I kept thinking again, there is a little girl there and there is a couple.
And the-- there is a choice between the little girl and other people get shot an myself too or go to a work camp.
So I decided to go to the work camp.
Do I continue?
We can take a break here.
Oh.
